title: Childhood Vaccination Schedule
oneLiner: Routine immunisation schedule for children in UK/Ireland; key to preventing serious infectious diseases and maintaining herd immunity.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Anaphylaxis post-vaccination** – rare but immediate. Single finding: stridor, wheeze, or hypotension within minutes of vaccination. Requires IM adrenaline 0.01 mL/kg (1:1000) and immediate resuscitation.  
- **Meningococcal sepsis in unvaccinated child** – non-blanching purpuric rash + fever + altered consciousness. Single finding: rash that does not fade with glass pressure. Must treat with IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) and urgent senior review; do not wait for blood cultures.
sections.Presentation:
- **Well child with incomplete vaccination** – parent brings red book, schedule gaps noted, may need catch-up.  
- **Fever or local reaction after vaccination** – mild fever, injection site redness/swelling, irritability (common; usually self-limiting).  
- **Parental anxiety/hesitancy** – concerns about vaccine safety, scheduling, or side effects.  
- **Child with contraindication to vaccination** – e.g. anaphylaxis to previous dose, immunocompromised, severe acute illness.  
- **Suspected vaccine-preventable disease** – e.g. rash + fever (measles), paroxysmal cough (pertussis), bulging fontanelle (Hib/meningococcal meningitis).  

- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Red book documentation – check vaccine dates and batch numbers.  
  - Injection site – tenderness, warmth, mild induration (normal).  
  - Signs of serious infection if unvaccinated – petechiae, neck stiffness, respiratory distress.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Incomplete vaccination (most likely)** – documented gaps in red book; no acute symptoms. Differentiator: review schedule and plot missed doses.  
- **Vaccine hesitancy** – parent expresses concerns; child may be fully or partially unvaccinated. Differentiator: explore safety myths with open questions.  
- **Contraindication** – history of anaphylaxis to previous dose, or current severe acute illness with fever >38.5°C. Differentiator: check allergy clinic letters, measure temperature.  
- **Vaccine-preventable disease** – child presents with symptoms of infection (rash, cough, fever) and is unvaccinated. Differentiator: acute symptoms + positive PCR or serology.  
- ****Splitter:** ** The presence of **fever >38.5°C** or **signs of systemic infection** separates simple schedule review from acute infection requiring immediate workup.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - Red book review – plot vaccines by age and dates.  
  - Temperature – if >38.5°C and child unwell, consider sepsis screen.  
  - Check for contraindications – ask about previous reactions, immunodeficiency.  
- **Bloods:**  
  - If suspected vaccine-preventable disease: full blood count, CRP, blood culture, PCR for suspected pathogen (e.g. meningococcus, measles).  
  - If anaphylaxis: mast cell tryptase (timed – within 1-2 hours of reaction, repeat at 24h).  
- **Imaging:**  
  - Not routinely indicated.  
  - If suspected meningitis: CT head only if focal neurology or GCS <13 before LP.  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Antibody titres – occasionally used to confirm immunity after catch-up (e.g. measles IgG).  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Check vaccination status in any child presenting with fever + rash or fever + sepsis.** Failure to identify an unvaccinated child at risk of vaccine-preventable disease is a serious omission.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Anaphylaxis** – IM adrenaline (1:1000) 0.01 mL/kg (max 0.5 mL); repeat every 5-10 min if no improvement. Call 2222 (paediatric crash team).  
  - **Meningococcal sepsis** – IV ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg (max 4 g) stat; fluids 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus; escalate to PICU.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - **Catch-up vaccination** – refer to UK schedule (see Drugs & Doses table).  
    - For delayed doses: follow [NICE guideline / local protocol] – do not restart schedule; give missed doses at earliest opportunity.  
    - For children >10 years: check if they have completed HPV/MenACWY/Tdap/IPV schedule.  
  - **Reassurance for mild reactions** – paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever/pain (avoid prophylactic antipyretics before vaccination except in high-risk groups).  
  - **Document all vaccines in red book and GP records** – include batch number, site, expiry date.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Refer to immunisation nurse or GP for ongoing catch-up.  
  - For children with contraindications – refer to paediatric immunology clinic.  
  - Provide written information (e.g. HSE/NHS leaflet) on schedule and side effects.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Not routinely required for vaccination review alone. If admitted for other reason, maintain normal diet and observations.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
**UK/Ireland childhood vaccination schedule (simplified – check local policy for exact ages/intervals)**  

| Age | Vaccines (Dose) | Route | Notes |
|-----|----------------|-------|-------|
| 8 weeks | DTaP/IPV/Hib/HepB (1), PCV (1), MenB (1), RV (1) | IM (DTaP/IPV/Hib/HepB, PCV, MenB); oral (RV) | RV – oral, must be given before 12 weeks ideally |
| 12 weeks | DTaP/IPV/Hib/HepB (2), MenB (2) | IM | Second dose |
| 16 weeks | DTaP/IPV/Hib/HepB (3), MenB (3) | IM | Third dose |
| 12-13 months | Hib/MenC (1), MMR (1), PCV (2) | IM | PCV – second dose |
| 12-13 months (continued) | MenB (booster) | IM | – |
| 3 years 4 months | MMR (2), DTaP/IPV (4) | IM | Pre-school booster |
| 12-13 years (girls & boys) | HPV (2 doses, 6-24 months apart) | IM | Gardasil 9 – 2 doses if <15 years, 3 if ≥15 |
| 14-15 years | Tdap/IPV, MenACWY | IM | Teenage booster |

**Key contraindications:**  
- Anaphylaxis to previous dose (or component, e.g. neomycin, gelatin).  
- Severe acute illness with fever >38.5°C (postpone until well).  
- Live vaccines (MMR, RV, varicella) – avoid in severe immunodeficiency (e.g. SCID, HIV with low CD4, chemotherapy).  

**Most dangerous dose error:**  
- **Giving a live vaccine (MMR, varicella, RV) to an immunocompromised child** – can cause disseminated disease. Always check immunodeficiency status before administration.  
- **Administering RV after 24 weeks (or 12 weeks in some schedules)** – increased risk of intussusception. Check age limits strictly.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Anaphylaxis (rare, 1 in 1 million doses).  
  - Febrile convulsion (post-MMR: 1 in 3000; usually benign).  
  - Injection site abscess or cellulitis (very rare).  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Intussusception (post-RV: ~1-2 in 100,000; weeks 1-7 after dose).  
  - Immune thrombocytopenia (post-MMR: 1 in 30,000).  
  - Long-term complications of missed vaccination: hospitalisation, mortality, outbreaks.
sections.Prognosis: - Vaccination is highly effective: >95% seroconversion for most antigens. Herd immunity prevents outbreaks. With complete schedule, child has minimal risk of vaccine-preventable diseases.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check the red book** – it is the legal record of vaccination. Document any vaccines you give in it immediately.  
- **If a child presents with fever + petechiae, ask vaccination status** – unvaccinated children are at higher risk of meningococcal sepsis.  
- **Use every contact to catch up** – if a child is admitted, review their vaccination record and offer missed vaccines (after ruling out acute contraindications).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Giving MMR or varicella vaccine to a child who is on high-dose steroids (<2 weeks after stopping) or has HIV with low CD4** – consult immunology first.  
- **Restarting the whole schedule if a child has missed a dose** – you only need to give the missed dose(s); do not restart the series.  
- **Forgetting to record batch number and route** – this is a medicolegal requirement. Write in red book and electronic record.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Severe allergic reaction (difficulty breathing, lip swelling, collapse) within 24 hours of vaccination.  
  - Fever >39°C lasting >48 hours or any seizure.  
  - Unusual crying, high-pitched scream, or inconsolable irritability >3 hours post-vaccination.  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP or health visitor for the next scheduled vaccination.  
  - If catch-up needed: make a clear plan (date, venue) before discharge.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / crash team for:**  
  - Anaphylaxis (any time after vaccination) – adrenaline + 2222.  
  - Meningococcal sepsis (hypotension, purpura fulminans, GCS <13) – call PICU/paediatric reg immediately.  
  - Suspected intussusception (post-RV: bilious vomiting, red-currant jelly stools, sausage-shaped mass) – urgent surgical review.

rawMarkdown:
## Childhood Vaccination Schedule  
**One-liner:** Routine immunisation schedule for children in UK/Ireland; key to preventing serious infectious diseases and maintaining herd immunity.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Anaphylaxis post-vaccination** – rare but immediate. Single finding: stridor, wheeze, or hypotension within minutes of vaccination. Requires IM adrenaline 0.01 mL/kg (1:1000) and immediate resuscitation.  
- **Meningococcal sepsis in unvaccinated child** – non-blanching purpuric rash + fever + altered consciousness. Single finding: rash that does not fade with glass pressure. Must treat with IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) and urgent senior review; do not wait for blood cultures.

### Presentation
- **Well child with incomplete vaccination** – parent brings red book, schedule gaps noted, may need catch-up.  
- **Fever or local reaction after vaccination** – mild fever, injection site redness/swelling, irritability (common; usually self-limiting).  
- **Parental anxiety/hesitancy** – concerns about vaccine safety, scheduling, or side effects.  
- **Child with contraindication to vaccination** – e.g. anaphylaxis to previous dose, immunocompromised, severe acute illness.  
- **Suspected vaccine-preventable disease** – e.g. rash + fever (measles), paroxysmal cough (pertussis), bulging fontanelle (Hib/meningococcal meningitis).  

- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Red book documentation – check vaccine dates and batch numbers.  
  - Injection site – tenderness, warmth, mild induration (normal).  
  - Signs of serious infection if unvaccinated – petechiae, neck stiffness, respiratory distress.  

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Incomplete vaccination (most likely)** – documented gaps in red book; no acute symptoms. Differentiator: review schedule and plot missed doses.  
- **Vaccine hesitancy** – parent expresses concerns; child may be fully or partially unvaccinated. Differentiator: explore safety myths with open questions.  
- **Contraindication** – history of anaphylaxis to previous dose, or current severe acute illness with fever >38.5°C. Differentiator: check allergy clinic letters, measure temperature.  
- **Vaccine-preventable disease** – child presents with symptoms of infection (rash, cough, fever) and is unvaccinated. Differentiator: acute symptoms + positive PCR or serology.  
- ****Splitter:** ** The presence of **fever >38.5°C** or **signs of systemic infection** separates simple schedule review from acute infection requiring immediate workup.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - Red book review – plot vaccines by age and dates.  
  - Temperature – if >38.5°C and child unwell, consider sepsis screen.  
  - Check for contraindications – ask about previous reactions, immunodeficiency.  
- **Bloods:**  
  - If suspected vaccine-preventable disease: full blood count, CRP, blood culture, PCR for suspected pathogen (e.g. meningococcus, measles).  
  - If anaphylaxis: mast cell tryptase (timed – within 1-2 hours of reaction, repeat at 24h).  
- **Imaging:**  
  - Not routinely indicated.  
  - If suspected meningitis: CT head only if focal neurology or GCS <13 before LP.  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Antibody titres – occasionally used to confirm immunity after catch-up (e.g. measles IgG).  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  - **Check vaccination status in any child presenting with fever + rash or fever + sepsis.** Failure to identify an unvaccinated child at risk of vaccine-preventable disease is a serious omission.  

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - **Anaphylaxis** – IM adrenaline (1:1000) 0.01 mL/kg (max 0.5 mL); repeat every 5-10 min if no improvement. Call 2222 (paediatric crash team).  
  - **Meningococcal sepsis** – IV ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg (max 4 g) stat; fluids 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus; escalate to PICU.  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - **Catch-up vaccination** – refer to UK schedule (see Drugs & Doses table).  
    - For delayed doses: follow [NICE guideline / local protocol] – do not restart schedule; give missed doses at earliest opportunity.  
    - For children >10 years: check if they have completed HPV/MenACWY/Tdap/IPV schedule.  
  - **Reassurance for mild reactions** – paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever/pain (avoid prophylactic antipyretics before vaccination except in high-risk groups).  
  - **Document all vaccines in red book and GP records** – include batch number, site, expiry date.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Refer to immunisation nurse or GP for ongoing catch-up.  
  - For children with contraindications – refer to paediatric immunology clinic.  
  - Provide written information (e.g. HSE/NHS leaflet) on schedule and side effects.  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Not routinely required for vaccination review alone. If admitted for other reason, maintain normal diet and observations.  

### Drugs & Doses  
**UK/Ireland childhood vaccination schedule (simplified – check local policy for exact ages/intervals)**  

| Age | Vaccines (Dose) | Route | Notes |
|-----|----------------|-------|-------|
| 8 weeks | DTaP/IPV/Hib/HepB (1), PCV (1), MenB (1), RV (1) | IM (DTaP/IPV/Hib/HepB, PCV, MenB); oral (RV) | RV – oral, must be given before 12 weeks ideally |
| 12 weeks | DTaP/IPV/Hib/HepB (2), MenB (2) | IM | Second dose |
| 16 weeks | DTaP/IPV/Hib/HepB (3), MenB (3) | IM | Third dose |
| 12-13 months | Hib/MenC (1), MMR (1), PCV (2) | IM | PCV – second dose |
| 12-13 months (continued) | MenB (booster) | IM | – |
| 3 years 4 months | MMR (2), DTaP/IPV (4) | IM | Pre-school booster |
| 12-13 years (girls & boys) | HPV (2 doses, 6-24 months apart) | IM | Gardasil 9 – 2 doses if <15 years, 3 if ≥15 |
| 14-15 years | Tdap/IPV, MenACWY | IM | Teenage booster |

**Key contraindications:**  
- Anaphylaxis to previous dose (or component, e.g. neomycin, gelatin).  
- Severe acute illness with fever >38.5°C (postpone until well).  
- Live vaccines (MMR, RV, varicella) – avoid in severe immunodeficiency (e.g. SCID, HIV with low CD4, chemotherapy).  

**Most dangerous dose error:**  
- **Giving a live vaccine (MMR, varicella, RV) to an immunocompromised child** – can cause disseminated disease. Always check immunodeficiency status before administration.  
- **Administering RV after 24 weeks (or 12 weeks in some schedules)** – increased risk of intussusception. Check age limits strictly.  

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Anaphylaxis (rare, 1 in 1 million doses).  
  - Febrile convulsion (post-MMR: 1 in 3000; usually benign).  
  - Injection site abscess or cellulitis (very rare).  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Intussusception (post-RV: ~1-2 in 100,000; weeks 1-7 after dose).  
  - Immune thrombocytopenia (post-MMR: 1 in 30,000).  
  - Long-term complications of missed vaccination: hospitalisation, mortality, outbreaks.  

### Prognosis
- Vaccination is highly effective: >95% seroconversion for most antigens. Herd immunity prevents outbreaks. With complete schedule, child has minimal risk of vaccine-preventable diseases.  

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check the red book** – it is the legal record of vaccination. Document any vaccines you give in it immediately.  
- **If a child presents with fever + petechiae, ask vaccination status** – unvaccinated children are at higher risk of meningococcal sepsis.  
- **Use every contact to catch up** – if a child is admitted, review their vaccination record and offer missed vaccines (after ruling out acute contraindications).  

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Giving MMR or varicella vaccine to a child who is on high-dose steroids (<2 weeks after stopping) or has HIV with low CD4** – consult immunology first.  
- **Restarting the whole schedule if a child has missed a dose** – you only need to give the missed dose(s); do not restart the series.  
- **Forgetting to record batch number and route** – this is a medicolegal requirement. Write in red book and electronic record.  

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Severe allergic reaction (difficulty breathing, lip swelling, collapse) within 24 hours of vaccination.  
  - Fever >39°C lasting >48 hours or any seizure.  
  - Unusual crying, high-pitched scream, or inconsolable irritability >3 hours post-vaccination.  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - GP or health visitor for the next scheduled vaccination.  
  - If catch-up needed: make a clear plan (date, venue) before discharge.  

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / crash team for:**  
  - Anaphylaxis (any time after vaccination) – adrenaline + 2222.  
  - Meningococcal sepsis (hypotension, purpura fulminans, GCS <13) – call PICU/paediatric reg immediately.  
  - Suspected intussusception (post-RV: bilious vomiting, red-currant jelly stools, sausage-shaped mass) – urgent surgical review.
